text: The robotic instruments grasp and manipulate the right fallopian tube, with the surgeon controlling the movements from a nearby console. The ovary appears normal in color and texture, while the fibroid uterus is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to seal and divide the ligaments and structures holding the uterus in place, carefully protecting the bladder and ureter. The bladder is pushed down and away from the operating area, revealing a transparent tissue layer. A vaginal cup supports the cervix, creating a visible hump that aids in safely repositioning the cervix away from the bladder. The surgeon gains control over the prominent blood vessels supplying the fibroid-laden uterus.

The surgeon ensures the bladder is safely pushed down and away from the operative field, moving from the right to the left side. Tissue is pushed away from the vaginal cup to protect the ureter, preventing injury. The surgeon identifies and skeletonizes hidden blood vessels, sealing them before proceeding to the left side where the fallopian tube has been previously removed.

The surgeon uses scissors to open the top of the vagina, revealing a green cup that serves as a guide and safety measure. The assistant prepares to remove the uterus, fallopian tube, and cervix through the vagina, placing a blue glove to maintain an airtight seal.

The surgeon sews up the top of the vagina, creating the vaginal cuff, using a tight, dissolvable stitch. The stitch is placed across the full thickness of the tissue, incorporating the ligaments that support the top of the vagina. The needle is removed, marking the end of the surgery.
